dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 4 for a possible seizure and head injury in the 2300 block of Larch Drive.
Hi, I'm John. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what brought you to call 911 today?
My neighbor just collapsed while cooking dinner, then hit his head when he fell again.
Mr. Smith, what exactly happened, if you can tell me?
He got stiff, jerked around, and then stopped. Next thing I saw, he was on the floor, confused, and bumped his head again.
Mr. Smith, are you awake and can you hear me?
He opens his eyes and moves slightly when I say his name.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me the name of the city we're in?
Richmond... I think.
Mr. Smith, can you squeeze my fingers for me?
squeezes fingers weakly* I think I can.
Mr. Smith, are you able to speak clearly right now?
Yes, but it's a bit hard to think clearly.
His breathing is unlabored and seems normal in rate and depth.
I count his respirations at 18 per minute now.
I'm going to check his pulse now. Mr. Smith, is that okay?
Pulse is at 103, regular and strong.
Rate is 18, depth is steady.
Blood pressure is 163 over 91.
We're doing a quick blood sugar check now. It's a small finger prick.
Oxygen saturation is 97% on room air.
You mentioned he had a seizure. Has anyone seen it?
Yes, he was eating, then froze up and collapsed in front of me.
Mr. Smith, let me test your responsiveness further. What is your name and age?
Thomas Smith, 44 years old.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me again the city we are in?
Richmond...
Please squeeze my fingers again as I ask. Ready?
squeezes fingers, a bit stronger than before
Where exactly are we now?
I think I'm in my kitchen in Richmond.
Mr. Smith, on a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is your pain right now?
There is no pain. I feel more confused than anything.
Mr. Smith, do you have any medical history I should know about?
I have primary hypertension, nothing else.
When was the last time you ate or drank?
A few minutes ago, he was eating chicken and rice.
Mr. Smith, we're going to shine a light in your eyes now. That's okay, right?
There's redness on the back of the head from when he hit the floor, no visible bleeding or serious injury.
Pupils are equal and reactive. Mr. Smith, can you see clearly now?
Yes, that's fine. No complaints there.
He is diaphoretic-cool to the touch, but sweating. Otherwise, skin looks unremarkable.
Partner, this situation fits into the medical-seizure protocol you think?
Absolutely, witnessed seizure with altered mental status. Protocol 3-12 should guide us.
Alright, we need the EKG set up. Let's do the 4-lead and 12-lead just now.
Applying the 4-lead now. Patient is male. Set up is done.
4-lead shows a normal rhythm. 12-lead EKG completed with no acute changes.
We'll transport you to Chippenham Hospital for further evaluation. That's the closest appropriate facility.
Mr. Smith, we'll help you get into the ambulance and secure you safely for the ride.
Let's recheck your vital signs now. Pulse is at 92 and regular. Respirator rate is 16, breathing smooth.
Blood pressure is now 159 over 100.
Oxygen saturation at 95% now. Glucose wasn't able to be obtained this time.
Repeating EKG now. Still showing sinus rhythm, no acute changes.
Mr. Smith, would you prefer IV for fluids or medications now?
If you need it, yes, but I'd rather wait unless it's necessary.
Understood. We'll proceed without the IV for now and monitor you closely.
Mr. Smith, we're placing you on the stretcher and using side rails to secure you during transport.
All systems and monitoring equipment are ready. Transport check done. Secure, ready to move.
Transport validation complete. All equipment locked and secure. Ready to begin moving to Chippenham.
Mr. Smith, please buckle up and be sure you're secured once we're loaded.
Chippenham ED team, we're transporting a 44-year-old male, Thomas Smith, following a witnessed seizure and head trauma.
No IV access placed due to patient refusal. Blood glucose not available this time.
GCS was 14 (Eye 4, Verbal 4, Motor 6). Initial vitals: BP 163/91, HR 103, RR 18, SpO2 97%.
Reassessment shows HR 92, RR 16, BP 159/100, SpO2 95%. Pupils equal and reactive. EKG is sinus rhythm, no STE or changes.
Redness but no serious injury or bleeding noted on head trauma site. No chest pain, shortness of breath, or abdominal pain reported.
Medical history: primary hypertension. No medications or allergies reported. Prior 4-lead and 12-lead ECG done: shows sinus tach with nonspecific T-wave abnormalities.
Transport secured with straps and side rails. Equipment prepped and patient monitored throughout transit. Ready to hand over.
